claim_text: AD vs CN diagnosis is decodable from OASIS structural features
head: linear probe

site_scanner.detail: single scanner/site — no acquisition confound to test

brain_age.detail: brain-age R2=0.39, MAE=4.9yr; effect retained 96% after brain-age-GAP control (Franke/Gaser standard), 19% under the stricter predicted-brain-age control

biomarker_anchor.detail: no plasma p-tau217 / GFAP coverage at usable n — cannot anchor (route to ADNI/EPAD)

replication.detail: single site/cohort — no held-out split available

caveats: Not corroborated: no molecular anchor (no open plasma data) and no passing cross-cohort replication — cannot be promoted.
Completeness: 3/5 gauntlet tests could not run; score is renormalized over the tests that did.
Verdict is provisional and conditional on the scanner/site distribution of any replication cohort.
Brain-age adjustment uses a proxy control; residual aging signal cannot be excluded.
Any biomarker anchor is complete-case only and may not generalize.
Leakage is bounded, not eliminated — see the published batch-effect audits (‘Batch Effects in Brain Foundation Model Embeddings,’ arXiv:2604.14441 (2026)).
A ‘robust enough for follow-up’ verdict is a licence to follow up, not to conclude — the referee promotes hypotheses, it does not confirm them.

The brain-age control is a proxy, not a gold standard: it is embedding-derived (this cohort's fit: R2=0.39, MAE=4.9yr), so residual generic-aging signal can survive the adjustment and masquerade as disease-specific.
There is no usable plasma p-tau217 / GFAP anchor here, so the molecular gate is unmet — the finding rests on imaging alone.
N is small (~98); at this size AUCs are noisy and a single site's idiosyncrasy can dominate — treat effect sizes as wide-interval estimates.

The leakage test audits the embeddings with the same linear-probe family it uses for the outcome, so it bounds — but cannot fully rule out — shared confounding; this is the mechanic quantified in ‘Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking: Auditing Cross-Encoder Attribute Transfer in EEG Foundation Models’ (arXiv:2606.09189 (2026)), which we cite rather than claim.

evidence_ledger.detail: single scanner/site — no acquisition confound to test
brain-age R2=0.39, MAE=4.9yr; effect retained 96% after brain-age-GAP control (Franke/Gaser standard), 19% under the stricter predicted-brain-age control
no plasma p-tau217 / GFAP coverage at usable n — cannot anchor (route to ADNI/EPAD)
single site/cohort — no held-out split available

narration: The claim “AD vs CN diagnosis is decodable from OASIS structural features” lands at “robust enough for follow-up” (84/100). The naive effect (AUC 0.76 separating AD from CN) is real on paper, but the referee's job is to ask what else could produce it. It sits below the promotion floor, so the honest read is that acquisition, demographics or generic aging can plausibly account for it, and it should not consume a scientist's quarter without stronger evidence. Where it stops being true: that a molecular anchor exists at all — with no p-tau217 / GFAP correlation measured here, the finding rests on imaging alone and would not survive a demand for orthogonal pathology. That the same frozen embeddings can predict scanner/site as well as outcome is documented prior art (“Batch Effects in Brain Foundation Model Embeddings,” arXiv:2604.14441 (2026)); NeuroAD Discovery Engine only runs the audit and issues the verdict.

critique: A ‘robust enough for follow-up’ verdict is a licence to follow up, not to conclude — the referee promotes hypotheses, it does not confirm them.

The brain-age control is a proxy, not a gold standard: it is embedding-derived (this cohort's fit: R2=0.39, MAE=4.9yr), so residual generic-aging signal can survive the adjustment and masquerade as disease-specific.
There is no usable plasma p-tau217 / GFAP anchor here, so the molecular gate is unmet — the finding rests on imaging alone.
N is small (~98); at this size AUCs are noisy and a single site's idiosyncrasy can dominate — treat effect sizes as wide-interval estimates.

The leakage test audits the embeddings with the same linear-probe family it uses for the outcome, so it bounds — but cannot fully rule out — shared confounding; this is the mechanic quantified in ‘Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking: Auditing Cross-Encoder Attribute Transfer in EEG Foundation Models’ (arXiv:2606.09189 (2026)), which we cite rather than claim.
revised_caveats: Verdict is provisional and conditional on the scanner/site distribution of any replication cohort.
Brain-age adjustment uses a proxy control; residual aging signal cannot be excluded.
Any biomarker anchor is complete-case only and may not generalize.
Leakage is bounded, not eliminated — see the published batch-effect audits (‘Batch Effects in Brain Foundation Model Embeddings,’ arXiv:2604.14441 (2026)).
